rationale: This structure is designed to mirror the primary specializations of academic and professional economists and financial researchers. It first splits the broad field into its two main components, Economics and Finance, and then subdivides each into established, practitioner-recognized areas. Finance is broken into its three pillars: Asset Pricing, Corporate Finance, and Banking. Economics is divided into core theory/methods (Macroeconomics, Microeconomic Theory, Econometrics) and a comprehensive set of major applied fields (e.g., Labor, Public, Development). This approach avoids a single, oversized 'Applied Microeconomics' category, providing more specific options that align with how researchers self-identify and how journals are specialized. We deliberately kept 'Macroeconomics' as a single large category, as modern research often integrates monetary, fiscal, and growth topics, making a further split difficult and less MECE.

subspecialties.name: Asset Pricing
subspecialties.tagline: Valuation of stocks, bonds, derivatives, and currencies in financial markets.
covers: Models of risk and return, market efficiency, and financial instrument valuation.
Factor models, derivatives pricing, market microstructure, portfolio theory, fixed income.
explicitly_excludes: Firm financing decisions (→ Corporate Finance)
Banking and financial institutions (→ Financial Intermediation & Banking)

subspecialties.name: Corporate Finance
subspecialties.tagline: How firms make financial decisions and are governed.
covers: Analysis of corporate investment, financing, and dividend policies.
Mergers & acquisitions (M&A), capital structure, corporate governance, venture capital.
explicitly_excludes: Valuation of publicly traded securities (→ Asset Pricing)
Regulation of financial institutions (→ Financial Intermediation & Banking)

subspecialties.name: Macroeconomics
subspecialties.tagline: Analysis of entire economies: growth, business cycles, inflation, and unemployment.
covers: Models of economic growth, monetary and fiscal policy, and business cycles.
DSGE models, inflation dynamics, sovereign debt, aggregate labor market fluctuations.
explicitly_excludes: International capital flows and exchange rates (→ International Economics)
Individual labor market studies (→ Labor Economics)

subspecialties.name: Development Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Economic transformation and welfare in low and middle-income countries.
covers: Study of poverty, inequality, institutions, and growth in developing nations.
Randomized controlled trials (RCTs), foreign aid, microfinance, institutional quality.
explicitly_excludes: Growth models for advanced economies (→ Macroeconomics)
International trade policy (→ International Economics)

subspecialties.name: Labor Economics
subspecialties.tagline: The study of labor markets, wages, employment, and education.
covers: Analysis of labor supply and demand, human capital, and wage determination.
Minimum wage effects, immigration, unemployment insurance, economics of education.
explicitly_excludes: Aggregate unemployment dynamics (→ Macroeconomics)
Social insurance program design (→ Public Economics)

subspecialties.name: Public Economics
subspecialties.tagline: The role of government in the economy, focusing on taxation and spending.
covers: Analysis of the effects of government taxation and expenditure.
Tax policy, social security, public goods, political economy, cost-benefit analysis.
explicitly_excludes: Monetary and central bank policy (→ Macroeconomics)
Antitrust and regulation of specific industries (→ Industrial Organization)

subspecialties.name: Health & Education Economics
subspecialties.tagline: The economics of healthcare markets and human capital formation.
covers: Analysis of health insurance, healthcare provision, and educational systems.
Returns to education, school choice, hospital competition, pharmaceutical pricing.
explicitly_excludes: General social insurance like unemployment (→ Public Economics)
General wage determination (→ Labor Economics)

subspecialties.name: Behavioral & Experimental Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Psychological foundations of economic decision-making, tested in labs and fields.
covers: Models of bounded rationality, social preferences, and time-inconsistent behavior.
Prospect theory, nudges, lab experiments on cooperation, field experiments.
explicitly_excludes: Standard rational-agent theory (→ Microeconomic Theory)
Applied work that uses behavioral insights but focuses on a specific field (→ e.g., Corporate Finance)

subspecialties.name: Industrial Organization
subspecialties.tagline: The study of firms, market structures, competition, and regulation.
covers: Analysis of market power, strategic interaction between firms, and antitrust policy.
Price discrimination, auctions, platform economics, network effects, antitrust.
explicitly_excludes: Firm's internal financing and investment decisions (→ Corporate Finance)
Economy-wide competition and growth (→ Macroeconomics)

subspecialties.name: International Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Economic interactions between countries, including trade and finance.
covers: Analysis of international trade patterns, exchange rates, and capital flows.
Trade agreements, tariffs, currency crises, balance of payments, globalization.
explicitly_excludes: Economic development of specific countries (→ Development Economics)
Domestic monetary/fiscal policy (→ Macroeconomics)

subspecialties.name: Financial Intermediation & Banking
subspecialties.tagline: The role of banks and other financial institutions in the economy.
covers: Study of banks, credit markets, financial crises, and regulation.
Bank runs, credit rationing, systemic risk, central banking operations, shadow banking.
explicitly_excludes: Pricing of financial assets traded in markets (→ Asset Pricing)
Non-financial corporate finance (→ Corporate Finance)

subspecialties.name: Environmental & Energy Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Economic approaches to pollution, natural resources, and energy markets.
covers: Analysis of environmental policy, resource management, and energy systems.
Carbon pricing, climate change economics, renewable energy, electricity markets.
explicitly_excludes: General government taxation and spending (→ Public Economics)
International treaties on non-environmental topics (→ International Economics)

subspecialties.name: Econometrics & Quantitative Methods
subspecialties.tagline: Development and application of statistical methods for economic data.
covers: Creation and analysis of statistical tools for estimating economic relationships.
Time series analysis, causal inference, instrumental variables, machine learning methods.
explicitly_excludes: Papers that apply existing methods to a substantive question (→ the relevant applied field)
Purely mathematical models without statistical application (→ Microeconomic Theory)

subspecialties.name: Microeconomic Theory
subspecialties.tagline: The mathematical foundations of economic behavior and market equilibrium.
covers: Formal modeling of individual choice, strategic interaction, and market design.
Game theory, mechanism design, general equilibrium theory, contract theory.
explicitly_excludes: Psychologically-based models of choice (→ Behavioral & Experimental Economics)
Statistical methods (→ Econometrics & Quantitative Methods)

subspecialties.name: Accounting
subspecialties.tagline: The measurement, processing, and communication of financial information.
covers: Research on financial reporting, disclosure, auditing, and managerial accounting.
Earnings management, information content of disclosures, auditor independence.
explicitly_excludes: Corporate investment and financing decisions (→ Corporate Finance)
The effect of news on stock prices (→ Asset Pricing)

subspecialties.name: Economic History
subspecialties.tagline: Using historical data and events to understand long-run economic processes.
covers: Quantitative analysis of historical economic phenomena and institutions.
The Great Depression, industrial revolution, historical origins of institutions.
explicitly_excludes: Analysis of current economic development (→ Development Economics)
History of economic thought (considered out of scope for this app)

uncovered_concerns: Urban & Real Estate Economics was considered but ultimately merged; urban papers often fit in Public or Labor, while real estate finance fits in Asset Pricing.
Law & Economics was excluded as it is highly interdisciplinary and papers often fit well into Public Economics or Industrial Organization.
Quantitative Finance (q-fin) was not made a separate category, as most academic work in this area is covered by Asset Pricing and Econometrics.
